repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
When a new textDocument/codeLens response arrives with unresolved lenses, on_win clears the existing codelens row before codeLens/resolve completes. This causes the displayed codelens text to flicker while typing.

## Solution
Keep the current virtual lines if any of the refreshed lenses are still unresolved. Clear the old virtual lines only when the line no longer has lenses or all its lenses are resolved.

A trade-off is that the user may temporarily see outdated codelenses. However, that's preferable to spamming updates on every refresh.

This is an adaptation of https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34888.

AI-assisted: Codex

comment_data.body: since this takes a `toprow, botrow`, could we debounce `Provider:on_win` and expand the "viewport" of `toprow, botrow` as needed? Then the final non-debounced call processes the expanded viewport.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -222,16 +222,29 @@ end
 ---@param toprow integer
 ---@param botrow integer
 function Provider:on_win(toprow, botrow)

comment_data.body:
Debouncing `on_win` could alleviate the flickering issue because it reduces the frequency of extmark clear/set.

But the flicker comes from exposing the intermediate state between where refreshed lenses are being resolved.
Even with debouncing, the final call still has to decide what to do for unresolved lenses.
If it clears the existing extmark at that point, users can still see flicker.

To avoid that, we need to keep the previously rendered extmark visible until the refreshed lens is resolved, and only then replace it atomically in a single redraw step. That is what this PR does.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -222,16 +222,29 @@ end
 ---@param toprow integer
 ---@param botrow integer
 function Provider:on_win(toprow, botrow)

comment_regular_data.body: This feature is on my roadmap, but I haven't figured out a good way to implement it yet. Looking at the code, I have some concerns about the current PR's implementation; it seems to have too much logic interspersed in various places.

> This feature is on my roadmap, but I haven't figured out a good way to implement it yet. Looking at the code, I have some concerns about the current PR's implementation; it seems to have too much logic interspersed in various places.

@ofseed is "your roadmap" documented somewhere? Else it might be unclear to other contributors what exactly you're planning on tackling and how.

> > This feature is on my roadmap, but I haven't figured out a good way to implement it yet. Looking at the code, I have some concerns about the current PR's implementation; it seems to have too much logic interspersed in various places.
> 
> 
> 
> @ofseed is "your roadmap" documented somewhere? Else it might be unclear to other contributors what exactly you're planning on tackling and how.

Thank you for the suggestion. I will open a tracking issue for this kind of case in the future. I only have a few vague ideas at the moment without any concrete approach yet, and I do not mean that this should become a blocker for other contributors.

So my main concern is that, personally, I am not very satisfied with some of the implementation choices here. For example, this newly added loop at the top of `on_win` appears to run in all cases, which means that even a viewport movement with no text change at all, such as moving up by one line, would still iterate over all extmarks in a buffer. By contrast, our existing caching mechanism only ever traverses the visible lines in the buffer, and it only operates on a line after that line’s content has changed; otherwise, it skips it directly. As a result, the performance hotspot would shift to the loop above.

> would still iterate over all extmarks in a buffer. 

it queries extmarks only in the visible range (`{ toprow, 0 }, { botrow, -1 }`)

> it queries extmarks only in the visible range (`{ toprow, 0 }, { botrow, -1 }`)

You are right. To clarify, I meant that when the viewport moves by one line, it still iterates through the extmarks within the visible buffer area, and the current caching mechanism finds that line and then iterates through the corresponding codelens when necessary.
I don't think this PR introduce meaningful overhead. The exisiting code already queries for (clears, to be precise) extmarks for each visible row.

Perhaps I am overthinking the performance aspect, as there may not be enough iterated extmarks to cause an issue, but the current code indeed does not attempt to clear extmarks on visible lines if we only move the viewport—which is the purpose of line 226, as shown below. It ensures that all code within this loop does not run when the document itself hasn't changed, performing only a version comparison.
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/2df2e72e0a3c5e44587a52698772d607101085c5/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/codelens.lua#L224-L228
Furthermore, this is another reason why I am not entirely satisfied with the changes in this PR; we already have a caching mechanism here to avoid redundant updates. Adding a patch here to prevent flickering doesn't seem like the best approach for the whole readability.

oh ok  I missed the version check. what do you think about the last commit? This moves the extmark query into the loop so it doesn't run when version hasn't changed.

The flickering problem this PR aims to fix is for the case where user is typing. So the current caching mechanism doesn't prevent this type of flcikering.

~A different issue but it seems this version-based skipping is wrong. I noticed that when I open a file for the first time, if the placeholder for the unresolved lens is displayed, it won't be replaced by the resolved lens if the buffer doesn't get modified.~ nvm. it seems rust-analyzer sometimes drops resolve requests.
Latest version looks simpler (avoids maintaining new state), LGTY @ofseed ?

It still has issues for me. For example, `get_extmark` occurs within each iteration of the client's codelens, which seems to mean that the logic here will no longer be applicable when multiple clients are connected. Additionally, it appears it can only resolve flickering that occurs when editing a specific line; when a user deletes or adds a line, the codelens flickering persists.

I'm not saying everything needs to be perfected all at once in this PR, but I suspect that the solution to the two problems above—especially the latter one—cannot be iteratively developed from the current version.

Thanks for that outline. As long as this doesn't regress anything substantially, it's an ok mitigation since the changes are isolated.

Let's agree that any further changes are blocked until a more holistic/long-term solution is found.

If this regresses something we can revert.
